IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—joint entity and relation extraction for KB construction—by proposing a novel tagging scheme that converts extraction into a tagging task (Sec. 3) and an end-to-end LSTM-LSTM-Bias model. Empirically it outperforms prior methods (reports a ~3% F1 gain over CoType on the NYT dataset), suggesting useful practical value. However the approach requires a predefined relation set and explicitly excludes overlapping relations (claimed as future work), and evaluation is limited to one distant-supervision dataset, so its influence is likely moderate rather than transformative.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is reasonably organized (intro → method → experiments) and the core tagging scheme is explained with a concrete example (BIES tags, Nt = 2*4*|R|+1) which aids comprehension. However, contributions are not stated clearly early and many passages are duplicated or out of order (repeated sentences/paragraphs in the introduction and experimental sections), figures/tables are referenced but not shown in the excerpt, and there are numerous editorial/grammatical issues that hinder flow. Overall the ideas are understandable but the presentation needs careful copy‑editing and reorganization to be fully clear.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The main claimed novelty is a new sequence-tagging formulation for joint entity+relation extraction (Sec. 3.1) that encodes BIES position, relation type and role into per-token tags, together with an LSTM decoder trained with a biased objective to favor relational labels (Sec. 3.2 and analysis of bias). These ideas are a clear, concrete re-formulation of the task compared to prior pipelined and joint approaches cited (e.g., Miwa & Bansal 2016; Ren et al. 2017), but are a relatively simple/engineering-level mapping and loss modification rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. Given the limitation that overlapping relations are not handled and the methods build on existing LSTM/sequence-tagging techniques, the contribution reads as a moderate (incremental) novelty rather than a highly original breakthrough.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This is squarely within top NLP/ML venue scope: joint entity and relation extraction is a core NLP problem and the paper proposes a novel tagging formulation (Sec. 3.1) and an end-to-end LSTM-based model with a biased objective (BiLSTM encoder + LSTM decoder, Sec. 3.2). The evaluation uses standard metrics (Precision/Recall/F1) on a public NYT distant-supervision dataset and compares against appropriate baselines (CoType, MultiR, LSTM-CRF, etc.) as expected for ACL/EMNLP-style work (Sec. 4.1). The paper is positioned with respect to relevant prior work (Miwa & Bansal 2016; Ren et al. 2017), and discusses practical limitations (e.g., overlapping relations not handled), indicating proper relevance and audience alignment.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments compare the proposed tagging model to a broad set of baselines (pipelined, joint, and end-to-end) on the NYT distant‑supervision dataset and report averages/stddevs, and include sensitivity analysis of the bias parameter α and a single-entity ratio analysis (Sections 4 and 5.2). However, evaluation is limited to a single dataset (NYT), the work explicitly excludes overlapping relations and leaves them for future work, and there is only partial ablation (e.g., bias vs. non-bias) but no deeper component-wise ablation or broader robustness checks. Reproducibility is aided by reporting model sizes, embeddings, and data source, but training details (optimizer, learning rate, batch size, preprocessing) are missing, and failure-mode analysis is minimal.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate baselines—pipelines (DS-logistic, LINE, FCM), joint methods (DS-Joint, MultiR, CoType) and end-to-end taggers (LSTM-CRF, LSTM-LSTM)—and evaluates on the same NYT dataset (Ren et al., 2017) with standard P/R/F metrics, using the same settings and reporting averages and stddevs (10 runs), which supports fair comparison. It also follows prior evaluation conventions (e.g. excluding “None” labels) and reuses CoType NER outputs for pipeline baselines. However, the paper largely asserts it "outperforms all other methods" (3% over CoType) without acknowledging any cases where prior work is comparable or better, and most analysis of differences focuses on the proposed bias objective and comparisons to the LSTM baselines rather than deeper error analyses versus all competing approaches.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states its main claim (a tagging scheme + biased LSTM model improves joint entity-relation extraction) and supports it with experiments against reasonable baselines (Section 4), but there are methodological gaps that weaken the evidence. The validation procedure is unclear/potentially problematic ("We create a validation set by randomly sampling 10% data from test set"), the test set is very small (395 sentences) and no statistical significance tests are reported despite reporting averages/stds, and the entity-pairing heuristic (combine same-relation entities by "nearest principle") is ad hoc and excludes overlapping relations (acknowledged). Mathematical content is minimal and correct where present (e.g. tag count Nt = 2*4*|R|+1), but the experimental issues and unsupported assumptions justify moderate rather than high confidence in the conclusions.
